feedback.text: Please consider including the protection of video games in relation to the discontinuation of support by publishers in the Digital Fairness Act. Video games have become an important cultural asset over the past 30 years, which, like classical literature and cinematographic works, have shaped entire generations and social movements. Unlike books and films, video games can disappear from one day to the next and remain closed to future social researchers. Currently, people buy video games without knowing when the publisher will switch off their DRM servers, so that the game in question no longer works. This applies not only to life-service and multiplayer games, but also to single players, which, in theory, can also function purely offline. We need a law that obliges publishers to set up a clear plan for the supporter of the games they sell. This means that the minimum duration of support must be clearly indicated on the side of the digital shop and the physical packaging. They must also publish methods that allow consumers to use and wait their games even after the end of the support, for example through a patch to switch off DRM protection. This concerns not only the protection of current consumers, but also the opportunities for sociologists and future historians to gain insight into our culture.
